title: Overpass-the-Hash / Pass-the-Ticket
briefing: On $TARGET a logon was recorded for $USER1 with logon type 9 (NewCredentials) via the seclogo logon process and runas.exe as the parent — the pattern produced by runas /netonly and by Mimikatz/Rubeus injecting a Kerberos ticket into a new logon session (overpass-the-hash / Pass-the-Ticket). The session is authenticating outward with credentials the attacker supplied, not the interactive user's. Identify the technique and why it is not a normal interactive logon.
dashboard_hint: Discover -> rule.id: 100136. Filter win.eventdata.logonType: 9 and win.eventdata.logonProcessName: seclogo. Type 9 means the session runs with explicit alternate credentials — a hallmark of ticket/hash injection. Correlate targetUserName = $USER1 on $TARGET.

questions.prompt: What does a type-9 (NewCredentials/seclogo) logon indicate here?
options: Alternate credentials injected into a session — overpass/Pass-the-Ticket
questions.answer: Alternate credentials injected into a session — overpass/Pass-the-Ticket
questions.explain: Type 9 runs the session under explicit alternate creds (runas /netonly, or a Kerberos ticket injected by Rubeus/Mimikatz) — the attacker's material, not the console user's.
All of the activity lands on $TARGET; the other hosts are unaffected.

questions.prompt: How does this differ from Pass-the-Hash (100135)?
options: It reuses a Kerberos ticket / alternate creds (type 9) rather than replaying an NTLM hash on a network logon
questions.answer: It reuses a Kerberos ticket / alternate creds (type 9) rather than replaying an NTLM hash on a network logon
questions.explain: PtH replays an NTLM hash (type 3, NTLM). Overpass/PtT injects a ticket into a new-credentials session (type 9, seclogo).
Ticket reuse (T1550.003) using valid-but-stolen credentials (T1078). No hash replay or encryption here.

options: Reset $USER1 and, if a TGT was forged, rotate the krbtgt password twice
Hunt for Rubeus/Mimikatz execution on the source host
Isolate $TARGET and review its outbound authentications
Assume any session opened by $USER1 may be attacker-controlled
Ignore — type 9 logons are always benign
answer: Reset $USER1 and, if a TGT was forged, rotate the krbtgt password twice
Hunt for Rubeus/Mimikatz execution on the source host
Isolate $TARGET and review its outbound authentications
Assume any session opened by $USER1 may be attacker-controlled
questions.explain: Ticket injection means the credential is already stolen: rotate it (and krbtgt twice if a golden/forged TGT is suspected), hunt the tooling, and distrust the session.
